title: CHRIS’s_version
text:
lapply() applies a function to every element in a list/vector/data frame and returns a list. Equivalently, we could use map() from the purrr package.
If we call ?lapply, we can see that it’s called with lapply(X, FUN, ...), where ... is optional arguments passed to the function FUN. Hence, we’d pass the following:


lapply(numeric_matrix, cor, method = "kendall")


Compared to baseR xapply() functions, purrr offer greater consistency in syntax regarding the selection and order of arguments each function takes. Another benefit is less ambiguity in the type of object returned; baseR functions may return different types based on the type of the input vector (like for sapply()).
A side effect function is a function which doesn’t simply return a transformation of the inputs. They may have no return at all. For example, the plot function takes data as an input and returns a plot object.
When functions are executed in R, a temporary environment is created. This means that ordinarily invalid variable names do not conflict with function names, because those functions are (generally) not part of the temporary environment.

title: CHRIS’s_version
CHRIS’s_version
text:
Generating RMSE Test Data:
Let’s run the given code to generate test data:

set.seed(10)
n <- 100
x <- runif(n)
resp <- 3 + 10 * x + rnorm(n)
pred <- predict(lm(resp ~ x), data.frame(x))

And test the RMSE function with it:

getRMSE(resp, pred)

[1] 0.9581677


Seems legit. We can replace a few values with NA and test passing the na.rm = TRUE argument:

missing_idx <- sample(length(resp), size = 2)
resp[missing_idx] <- NA_real_

getRMSE(resp, pred)

[1] NA

getRMSE(resp, pred, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 0.9659906


Great! It looks like we can pass arguments to the mean() function!

title: CHRIS’s_version
CHRIS’s_version
text:
Wrapper Function
Last, we write a wrapper function to call one or both of the helper functions we just wrote:

getMetric <- function(resp, pred, metrics, ...) {
  valid_metrics <- c("RMSE", "MAE")
  
  if(length(setdiff(metrics, valid_metrics)) > 0) {
    stop("Error: Invalid metrics specified. Specify at least one of: RMSE, MAE")
  }
  
  output <- numeric()
  
  if("RMSE" %in% metrics) {
    output["RMSE"] <- getRMSE(resp, pred, ...)
  }
  if("MAE" %in% metrics) {
    output["MAE"] <- getMAE(resp, pred, ...)
  }
  
  output
}

Again, generate data to test:

set.seed(10)
n <- 100
x <- runif(n)
resp <- 3 + 10 * x + rnorm(n)
pred <- predict(lm(resp ~ x), data.frame(x))

Test querying metrics separately and together:

getMetric(resp, pred, "MAE")

      MAE 
0.9030933 

getMetric(resp, pred, "RMSE")

     RMSE 
0.9581677 

getMetric(resp, pred, c("MAE", "RMSE"))

     RMSE       MAE 
0.9581677 0.9030933 


Let’s add some missingness and retest:

missing_idx <- sample(length(resp), size = 2)
resp[missing_idx] <- NA_real_

getMetric(resp, pred, c("MAE", "RMSE"))

RMSE  MAE 
  NA   NA 

getMetric(resp, pred, c("MAE", "RMSE"), na.rm = TRUE)

     RMSE       MAE 
0.9659906 0.9078670 


Excellent! Lastly, we should see what happens if we pass a data frame instead of a vector:

resp_df <- data.frame(resp)

getMetric(resp_df, pred, "MAE")

Error in getMAE(resp, pred, ...): Error: Reponse isn't a vector


Sweet! The data validation we did earlier still works when called in the wrapper function.
